lessons.title: The Digital Multimeter: Your First Tool
lessons.content:
## Hook

Electricity is invisible. You cannot see voltage or current the way you can see water in a pipe. To build or fix electronics, you need tools that let you *see* electricity. A **digital multimeter (DMM)** is the first and most important of these tools. It is to a circuit what a thermometer is to a patient: a quick, cheap way to take a vital sign.

## Build

A DMM measures several things, and you turn a dial to pick which:

- **Voltage (V)** — electrical "pressure," the push between two points. Measured in volts.
- **Current (I)** — the *flow* of electricity, how much is moving. Measured in amps.
- **Resistance (R)** — how much a part fights the flow. Measured in ohms ($\Omega$).
- **Continuity** — a simple yes/no: are these two points connected? The meter beeps if they are. Great for finding broken wires.

The golden rule is **how you connect it**:

- To measure **voltage**, touch the two probes *across* the part (in **parallel**), like reading the pressure on both sides of a valve. A voltmeter has very high resistance, so almost no current detours into it.
- To measure **current**, you must break the circuit and put the meter *in line* (in **series**), so all the flow passes through it. A current meter (ammeter) has very low resistance.

A common beginner mistake: leaving the meter in current mode (low resistance) and touching it across a battery. That is nearly a short circuit and can blow the meter's fuse.

## Recap

A DMM measures voltage, current, resistance, and continuity. Voltage probes go *across* a part (parallel); current means the meter goes *in line* (series). Pick the right function and connection before you touch anything.

items.prompt: What does a multimeter let you do that your eyes cannot?
choices: See the color of a wire
See invisible electrical quantities like voltage and current
Measure the temperature of a room
Count the number of components on a board
items.explanation: Electricity is invisible. A multimeter turns hidden quantities (voltage, current, resistance) into numbers you can read, the way a thermometer turns invisible body heat into a number.

items.prompt: Voltage is best described in everyday words as:
choices: The electrical 'pressure' or push between two points
The number of electrons in a wire
How hot a component gets
The speed of a signal
items.explanation: Voltage is the push or 'pressure' between two points, like water pressure across a valve. Current is the actual flow that this pressure causes.

items.prompt: To measure the voltage across a resistor, how do you connect the meter?
choices: In line with the resistor, after breaking the circuit
Across the resistor (in parallel), touching both ends
It does not matter how you connect it
Only to the metal case of the device
items.explanation: Voltage is measured ACROSS a part, in parallel. You touch the two probes to the two ends without breaking anything. A voltmeter's high resistance means it barely disturbs the circuit.

items.prompt: To measure the current flowing through a wire, you must:
choices: Touch both probes to the same point
Hold the probes near the wire without touching it
Break the circuit and put the meter in line (in series) so all the flow goes through it
Measure the voltage instead and assume it is the same
items.explanation: Current is the flow, so the meter must be IN the path (series): you break the circuit and insert the meter so every bit of current passes through it. A current meter has very low resistance to disturb the flow as little as possible.

items.prompt: The continuity setting (the one that beeps) is most useful for:
choices: Measuring exact resistance values
Quickly checking whether two points are electrically connected, like finding a broken wire
Reading the voltage of a battery
Measuring how fast a signal changes
items.explanation: Continuity is a fast yes/no test: the meter beeps if the two points are connected (very low resistance). It is the go-to for finding broken wires, blown fuses, or confirming a solder joint.

items.prompt: A voltmeter is designed to have VERY HIGH internal resistance. Why is that a good thing?
choices: So it draws a lot of current to get a strong reading
So almost no current detours into the meter, leaving the circuit nearly undisturbed
So it can also measure resistance at the same time
So it heats up and reads faster
items.explanation: A voltmeter sits across the circuit. High internal resistance means it siphons off almost no current, so it measures the voltage without significantly changing it. (No real meter is perfect, as the next lesson explains.)

items.prompt: You leave the meter set to measure CURRENT (low resistance) and touch its probes directly across a battery. What is likely to happen?
choices: Nothing; the meter automatically corrects itself
You read the correct voltage
You create a near-short circuit, possibly blowing the meter's fuse
The battery charges up
items.explanation: In current mode the meter is nearly a wire (very low resistance). Placing it directly across a battery is almost a dead short, dumping huge current and blowing the meter's protective fuse. Always pick the right function first.

items.prompt: Which job calls for the resistance (ohms) setting rather than voltage or current?
choices: Finding out the value of a loose resistor that is not connected to anything
Checking the 9 V on a live battery
Seeing how much current a running motor draws
Watching a signal change shape over time
items.explanation: Resistance mode sends a tiny test current through a part and reads how much it resists. Use it on parts that are powered off and ideally removed from the circuit. Measuring resistance in a live or connected circuit gives misleading numbers.

items.prompt: A friend says 'just measure current the same easy way you measure voltage, by touching across the part.' Why is that wrong?
choices: Current and voltage are actually the same thing
Current must flow THROUGH the meter, so the meter has to be inserted in line (series), not laid across
You can never measure current with a multimeter
Touching across a part is dangerous but gives the right current anyway
items.explanation: Voltage is measured across (parallel); current is measured in line (series). To read flow, every bit of current must travel through the meter, which means breaking the circuit and inserting it. This is the single most common beginner mix-up.

lessons.title: How a Multimeter Can Fool You
lessons.content:
## Hook

A multimeter does not lie randomly — it fools you in *predictable* ways. Knowing those ways is the difference between trusting a number and being misled by it. The first big idea: the very act of measuring changes the thing you measure, a little.

## Build

**Loading (the meter steals a bit).** A voltmeter has high but not infinite resistance — commonly $10\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$ (ten million ohms). When the circuit you are probing is also high-resistance, the meter and the circuit share the voltage like two resistors in a divider, and the meter reads a bit low. Example: a $1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$ source measured with a $10\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$ meter reads $\frac{10}{11}\approx 91\%$ of the true value. That ~9% droop is **loading error**, not a broken meter. It only matters when the circuit's own resistance is large.

**Burden voltage (the current-meter tax).** To measure current, the meter inserts a small resistor and reads the voltage across it. That small voltage — the **burden voltage** — is stolen from the circuit. In a tiny low-voltage circuit it can noticeably reduce the very current you wanted to measure.

**True-RMS vs. average.** For wavy AC signals, a cheap meter assumes the wave is a perfect sine and applies a fixed fudge factor. Feed it a non-sine (a square wave, a dimmer's chopped waveform) and it reads wrong. A **true-RMS** meter actually computes the right value for *any* shape.

**Resolution is not accuracy.** More digits on the display (resolution) just means finer steps. **Accuracy** is how close to the truth it is, quoted like $\pm(\text{percent of reading} + \text{a few counts})$. A meter can show many digits and still be a little off.

## Recap

Meters mislead predictably: loading on high-resistance nodes, burden voltage when measuring small currents, wrong AC readings on non-sines without true-RMS, and lots of digits that are not the same as accuracy.

items.prompt: What is 'loading' when measuring voltage?
choices: The meter adds extra voltage to the circuit
The meter's resistance forms a divider with a high-resistance circuit, making it read slightly low
The meter needs to warm up before it is accurate
The battery inside the meter is low
items.explanation: A real voltmeter has high but finite resistance. On a high-resistance node it shares the voltage like a divider, so it reads a little low. This is loading error, and it grows as the circuit's own resistance approaches the meter's.

items.prompt: You measure a 1.000 V source whose internal resistance is 1 MΩ using a 10 MΩ meter. About what does it read?
items.explanation: The 10 MΩ meter and the 1 MΩ source act as a divider: 10/(10+1) = 0.909. So it reads about 0.909 V. This is loading, not a fault. On a low-resistance source the same meter would read essentially correctly.

items.prompt: Loading error is most likely to be a problem when:
choices: The circuit you measure has very LOW resistance, like a battery terminal
The circuit you measure has very HIGH resistance, comparable to the meter's own
You are measuring resistance
The signal is a perfect sine wave
items.explanation: Loading bites when the source resistance is large compared to the meter's input resistance. On low-resistance sources (a battery, a power rail) a 10 MΩ meter barely disturbs anything.

items.prompt: What is 'burden voltage'?
choices: The voltage a meter adds to make readings stronger
The small voltage dropped across the current meter's internal resistor, stolen from the circuit
The voltage of the meter's own battery
The maximum voltage a meter can survive
items.explanation: To read current, the meter passes it through a small internal resistor and measures the voltage across it. That voltage, the burden voltage, is subtracted from the circuit and can starve a small low-voltage circuit of the very current you are measuring.

items.prompt: A cheap 'average-responding' AC meter is most trustworthy on which signal?
choices: A clean sine wave like the mains
A square wave
A chopped waveform from a light dimmer
A noisy spiky pulse
items.explanation: An average-responding meter assumes the signal is a perfect sine and applies a fixed correction factor. That is only correct for sine waves. On squares, chopped, or spiky signals it reads wrong; a true-RMS meter handles any shape.

items.prompt: Why would you spend more on a 'true-RMS' multimeter?
choices: It has a brighter display
It computes the correct AC value for ANY waveform shape, not just sines
It measures voltage faster
It never needs calibration
items.explanation: True-RMS meters actually compute the real heating-equivalent value of the waveform, so they stay correct on squares, chopped dimmer signals, and other non-sines where an average-responding meter is fooled.

items.prompt: A meter shows '1.2345 V' with five digits. A friend says 'so it is accurate to the last digit.' Why might that be wrong?
choices: Five digits always means perfect accuracy
Resolution (number of digits) is not the same as accuracy (closeness to truth); the reading can still be off
The meter is broken if it shows five digits
More digits always means less accuracy
items.explanation: Resolution is just how finely the display steps. Accuracy is how close to the true value it is, quoted as plus/minus (percent of reading + counts). A meter can show many digits yet still read slightly off.

items.prompt: A meter is spec'd at ±(0.5% of reading + 3 counts) and reads 1.000 V on a range where 1 count = 1 mV. The worst-case uncertainty is about:
choices: ±3 mV
±5 mV
±8 mV
items.explanation: 0.5% of 1.000 V = 5 mV, plus 3 counts = 3 mV, totaling about 8 mV worst case. The 'counts' term is why low readings on a high range are proportionally less trustworthy.

items.prompt: You measure a tiny current in a delicate low-voltage circuit and the circuit behaves differently while the meter is connected. The most likely culprit is:
choices: Loading error from the voltmeter
Burden voltage from the current meter robbing the circuit
Aliasing
The continuity beeper
items.explanation: When measuring current in a small low-voltage circuit, the burden voltage across the meter's internal resistor is subtracted from the circuit and can change its behavior. Using a higher current range (smaller internal resistor) reduces burden voltage.

lessons.title: The Oscilloscope: Seeing a Signal's Shape
lessons.content:
## Hook

A multimeter gives you one number. But many signals are not a single number — they wiggle, pulse, and ring over time. An **oscilloscope** ('scope') draws a graph of voltage over time, so you can actually *see* the shape of a signal. If the DMM is a thermometer, the scope is a heart-rate monitor showing the whole waveform.

## Build

The screen is a grid. The **horizontal** axis is time and the **vertical** axis is voltage. You set how much each square is worth: **time/div** (e.g. 1 ms per square) and **volts/div** (e.g. 0.5 V per square). Count the squares and you read the signal's size and timing.

Three things decide whether you see the truth:

**Bandwidth.** A scope can only follow changes up to a certain speed, its **bandwidth**. A fast, sharp edge needs lots of bandwidth. The rule of thumb links the fastest edge (its 10%-to-90% **rise time** $t_r$) to bandwidth: $$BW \approx \frac{0.35}{t_r}$$ A faster edge (smaller $t_r$) needs more bandwidth. If the scope is too slow, sharp edges look soft and rounded.

**Sample rate and aliasing.** A digital scope takes snapshots (samples). If it samples too slowly for a fast signal, it can draw a totally fake, slow-looking wave that is not really there — this trap is called **aliasing**. The cure is to sample much faster than the signal, or limit the bandwidth.

**10x probes.** A plain (1x) probe loads the circuit and can distort fast signals. A **10x probe** divides the signal by ten but disturbs the circuit far less. It has a tiny adjustment screw (**compensation**): probe a 1 kHz square wave and tweak it until the corners look square, not rounded or peaked.

## Recap

A scope graphs voltage vs. time; you set time/div and volts/div. Watch three things: enough bandwidth for sharp edges ($BW \approx 0.35/t_r$), enough sample rate to avoid aliasing, and a properly compensated 10x probe to avoid loading and distortion.

items.prompt: What does an oscilloscope show that a multimeter does not?
choices: A single average number for voltage
The shape of a signal as it changes over time
The resistance of a part
Whether two points are connected
items.explanation: A scope draws voltage on the vertical axis vs. time on the horizontal axis, so you see the actual shape: edges, ringing, pulses. A multimeter collapses all that into one number.

items.prompt: On a scope screen, what do the horizontal and vertical axes represent?
choices: Horizontal = voltage, vertical = current
Horizontal = time, vertical = voltage
Horizontal = frequency, vertical = power
Both axes show voltage
items.explanation: Horizontal is time (set by time/div) and vertical is voltage (set by volts/div). Counting grid squares times the per-square setting gives you the signal's timing and amplitude.

items.prompt: If each vertical square is 'volts/div = 0.5 V' and a wave peaks 3 squares above center, its peak voltage is:
choices: 3 V
6 V
items.explanation: 3 squares x 0.5 V per square = 1.5 V. Reading a scope is just counting divisions and multiplying by the per-division setting.

items.prompt: A scope's 'bandwidth' tells you:
choices: How many channels it has
The fastest signal changes it can follow before they look soft and rounded
How bright the screen is
How many volts it can survive
items.explanation: Bandwidth is the speed limit of the scope. Signal changes faster than the bandwidth get smoothed out, so sharp edges appear rounded and amplitudes shrink. You need bandwidth comfortably above your signal's fastest features.

items.prompt: Using BW ≈ 0.35/t_r, a signal with a 1 ns rise time needs a scope bandwidth of at least about:
choices: 35 kHz
3.5 MHz
350 MHz
35 GHz
items.explanation: 0.35 / 1 ns = 350 MHz just to match the edge. In practice you want several times that for a faithful picture, because the scope's own speed limit otherwise rounds the edge.

items.prompt: What is 'aliasing' on a digital scope?
choices: The screen getting too bright
Sampling too slowly, so a fast signal is drawn as a fake slow wave that is not really there
Two probes touching
The trigger failing to start
items.explanation: A digital scope captures snapshots. Too few snapshots of a fast signal makes it draw a convincing but false low-frequency wave. The cure: sample much faster than the signal, or enable a bandwidth limit/anti-alias setting.

items.prompt: You suspect the slow-looking wave on your scope is an aliasing artifact. A good way to confirm is:
choices: Change the timebase / sample rate; a real signal's frequency stays put, an alias jumps around
Increase the screen brightness
Switch the meter to continuity
Disconnect the ground lead
items.explanation: A genuine signal keeps the same frequency as you change the sample rate or timebase. An aliased 'ghost' shifts its apparent frequency because it is an artifact of undersampling, not a real tone.

items.prompt: Why use a 10x probe instead of a plain 1x probe on a fast or high-resistance signal?
choices: It makes the signal ten times bigger
It disturbs (loads) the circuit far less, at the cost of dividing the signal by ten
It removes the need for triggering
It increases the scope's bandwidth tenfold
items.explanation: A 10x probe presents much higher resistance and lower capacitance to the circuit, so it loads sensitive nodes far less. The trade is a 10x smaller signal at the scope, which the scope rescales when told the probe ratio.

items.prompt: You probe a 1 kHz square wave through a 10x probe and the corners look rounded instead of square. What should you do?
choices: Nothing; rounded corners are normal
Adjust the probe's compensation trimmer until the corners are square
Increase the scope's voltage range
Switch to a 1x probe permanently
items.explanation: Rounded (or peaked) corners on the cal square wave mean the 10x probe is mis-compensated. Turn the small trimmer until the corners are flat and square. This is the standard probe-compensation step before any measurement.

items.prompt: A 100 MHz scope is asked to show a signal whose edges are far faster than 100 MHz worth of bandwidth. The displayed edges will look:
choices: Sharper than reality
Softer and slower than reality, because the scope cannot keep up
Exactly correct
Inverted
items.explanation: When the signal's edges are faster than the scope can follow, the scope rounds them off, showing a slower rise time than the real signal has. To see fast edges faithfully, pick a scope with bandwidth well above the signal.

lessons.title: Function Generators & Bench Supplies
lessons.content:
## Hook

So far the tools have *watched* a circuit. Two tools instead *feed* a circuit: a **function generator** makes test signals (sines, squares, ramps) so you can poke a circuit and see how it responds, and a **bench power supply** delivers clean, adjustable power. Both have one famous trap each that fools beginners.

## Build

**The function generator and the 50-ohm 2x gotcha.** A function generator does not have a perfect output — it behaves as if there is a $50\,\Omega$ resistor in series with its source. Its amplitude dial is calibrated assuming you connect it to a matching $50\,\Omega$ load, which forms a 2-to-1 divider and halves the output. But a scope input is high resistance (about $1\,\mathrm{M}\Omega$), so there is almost no divider — and you get **twice** the voltage you dialed in. Set $1\,\mathrm{V}$, see $2\,\mathrm{V}$. The fix: tell the generator's menu it is driving a 'High-Z' load (it then halves its internal setting to compensate), or actually terminate the line with a $50\,\Omega$ resistor.

An **arbitrary** waveform generator is the same idea but can play any custom shape you draw, not just standard waves.

**The bench supply: CV and CC.** A good supply has two limits. In **constant voltage (CV)** mode it holds the voltage you set and supplies whatever current the load needs. But you also set a **current limit**; if the load tries to draw more, the supply switches to **constant current (CC)** mode, holding current steady and *letting the voltage sag*. This is a safety net: set a sensible current limit and a wiring short or a backwards part draws a safe, capped current instead of frying.

## Recap

A function generator's amplitude assumes a 50 Ω load; into a high-resistance scope you see double, so set High-Z or terminate in 50 Ω. A bench supply holds voltage (CV) until the current limit is hit, then switches to constant current (CC) to protect your circuit.

items.prompt: What is the main purpose of a function generator?
choices: To measure the resistance of a part
To produce test signals (sines, squares, ramps) to drive a circuit and see how it responds
To display a signal's shape over time
To provide steady DC power only
items.explanation: A function generator is a signal source: it makes controllable waveforms you inject into a circuit so you can observe the response (on a scope, say). It does not measure; it stimulates.

items.prompt: You set a function generator to 1 V amplitude, connect it to a high-resistance scope input, and see 2 V. Why?
choices: The scope is broken
The generator's amplitude assumes a 50 Ω load; into a high-Z scope there is no divider, so you see double
Aliasing doubled the reading
The probe is set to 10x
items.explanation: The generator's dial is calibrated for a matching 50 Ω load, which would halve its raw output via a 2:1 divider. A high-resistance scope provides no such divider, so the full (doubled) voltage appears. This is the classic 50-ohm gotcha.

items.prompt: The simplest way to make the function generator's dial match what you actually see on a high-impedance scope is to:
choices: Set the generator's output-load menu to 'High-Z'
Turn the amplitude up to maximum
Switch the scope to AC coupling
Use a 1x probe instead of 10x
items.explanation: Telling the generator it drives a High-Z load makes it halve its internal output so the displayed value matches reality. Alternatively, physically terminate the line in 50 ohms so the assumed divider actually exists.

items.prompt: An 'arbitrary' waveform generator differs from a basic function generator in that it can:
choices: Only make sine waves
Play any custom waveform shape you define, not just standard sines/squares/ramps
Measure current
Supply high-power DC
items.explanation: An arbitrary waveform generator stores and replays any shape you draw or upload, useful for recreating real-world or unusual signals. A plain function generator is limited to standard waveform types.

items.prompt: On a bench power supply, 'constant voltage (CV)' mode means:
choices: It holds the set voltage and supplies whatever current the load needs (up to the limit)
It holds a fixed current no matter what
It outputs zero volts
It measures the load's resistance
items.explanation: In CV mode the supply keeps the voltage you dialed in and provides as much current as the load draws, as long as that current stays under the current limit you set.

items.prompt: When does a bench supply switch from constant voltage (CV) to constant current (CC) mode?
choices: When the load tries to draw more current than the set current limit
When you press the power button
Whenever the voltage is above 5 V
Only when measuring resistance
items.explanation: If the load demands more than the set current limit, the supply caps the current and lets the voltage sag to whatever holds that current. That handoff from CV to CC is what protects your circuit.

items.prompt: Why is setting a sensible current limit on a bench supply a good safety habit?
choices: It makes the voltage more accurate
If you have a wiring short or a backwards part, the supply caps the current instead of dumping enough to burn your circuit
It speeds up the measurement
It prevents aliasing
items.explanation: A current limit turns a catastrophic short into a harmless CC condition: the supply holds current at the cap and the voltage drops. Many a board has been saved by a current limit set before powering up.

items.prompt: You forgot the 50-ohm setting and your circuit is getting twice the drive voltage you intended. The most direct fix is to:
choices: Replace the function generator
Set the generator to High-Z mode, or terminate its output in 50 Ω, so the amplitude matches reality
Lower the scope's volts/div
Switch the bench supply to CC mode
items.explanation: The doubling comes purely from the load-impedance assumption. Correct it at the generator (set the actual load to High-Z) or make the assumption true (terminate in 50 ohms). Nothing is wrong with the hardware.

items.prompt: Which tool would you reach for to 'wiggle' a circuit's input and watch the output respond?
choices: A multimeter on the resistance setting
A function generator (to drive) together with an oscilloscope (to watch)
A continuity beeper
A bench supply alone
items.explanation: Stimulus-response testing pairs a function generator (the stimulus) with a scope (the observation). The DMM gives static numbers, and the supply only powers the circuit; neither shows you a dynamic response.

lessons.title: Spectrum Analyzers, Calibration & Trusting Your Tools
lessons.content:
## Hook

A scope shows a signal's *shape* over time. A **spectrum analyzer** answers a different question: what *frequencies* (tones) are hidden inside that signal? Every signal can be seen as a recipe of pure tones added together. The spectrum analyzer shows you that recipe — and it is how engineers hunt down noise, hum, and distortion.

## Build

**The frequency view.** On a spectrum analyzer the horizontal axis is **frequency** (not time) and the vertical axis is **power** (how strong each tone is, usually in dB). A pure sine shows a single tall spike. A square wave shows a tall spike at its base frequency (the **fundamental**) plus smaller spikes at multiples — these extra tones are **harmonics**, and they are what give a signal its 'edge.' Unwanted harmonics reveal distortion.

**The noise floor.** Down at the bottom is a fuzzy carpet of background noise: the **noise floor**. Any tone you want to measure must poke up above it. Narrowing the analyzer's resolution setting lowers the noise floor so you can spot tiny tones — at the cost of a slower sweep.

**Calibration and uncertainty.** No instrument is perfectly accurate. **Calibration** means periodically checking a tool against a trusted reference standard and recording when it was last verified. Every measurement carries some **uncertainty** — a small plus/minus band. When several independent error sources combine, they add not by simple sum but in **quadrature** (root-sum-square): $$u_\mathrm{total} = \sqrt{u_1^2 + u_2^2 + \dots}$$ So a 2% and a 1.5% error combine to about 2.5%, not 3.5%. The professional habit is simple: trust, but verify against a known reference.

## Recap

A spectrum analyzer shows power vs. frequency: a fundamental plus harmonics, sitting above a noise floor. Calibration keeps tools honest against a reference, and independent uncertainties combine in quadrature (root-sum-square), not by adding directly.

items.prompt: What question does a spectrum analyzer answer that an oscilloscope does not?
choices: What does the signal's shape look like over time?
What frequencies (tones) are present inside the signal, and how strong is each?
Is this wire connected to that one?
What is the resistance of this part?
items.explanation: A scope shows shape vs. time. A spectrum analyzer shows power vs. frequency: it breaks the signal into its component tones, revealing harmonics, noise, and interference that are hard to see in the time view.

items.prompt: On a spectrum analyzer display, the two axes are:
choices: Horizontal = time, vertical = voltage
Horizontal = frequency, vertical = power (strength of each tone)
Horizontal = resistance, vertical = current
Both axes show frequency
items.explanation: Horizontal is frequency and vertical is power (often in dB). Each vertical spike marks a tone present in the signal and how strong it is.

items.prompt: A pure, perfect sine wave appears on a spectrum analyzer as:
choices: A single tall spike at one frequency
Many evenly spaced spikes
A flat horizontal line
A rounded hill
items.explanation: A pure sine contains exactly one frequency, so it shows up as a single spike. Extra spikes would mean the sine is not pure, indicating distortion or added harmonics.

items.prompt: What are 'harmonics'?
choices: Random noise with no pattern
Extra tones at whole-number multiples of the fundamental frequency
The DC level of a signal
The meter's internal resistance
items.explanation: Harmonics are tones at integer multiples of the fundamental (2x, 3x, ...). A square wave is rich in them. Unexpected harmonics in what should be a clean sine reveal distortion in a circuit.

items.prompt: The 'noise floor' on a spectrum analyzer is:
choices: The strongest tone in the signal
The background carpet of noise; tones must rise above it to be seen
The frequency axis
The maximum power the analyzer can read
items.explanation: The noise floor is the fuzzy baseline of background noise. Any tone you want to measure has to poke up above it. Tones buried below the noise floor cannot be distinguished from the noise.

items.prompt: You want to spot a very tiny tone that is nearly buried in the noise. A reasonable move is to:
choices: Raise the noise floor
Narrow the analyzer's resolution setting to lower the noise floor (accepting a slower sweep)
Switch the analyzer to time domain
Use the continuity beeper
items.explanation: Narrowing the resolution bandwidth lowers the noise floor, letting small tones emerge above it. The trade-off is a slower sweep. This is the standard technique for digging weak signals out of noise.

items.prompt: What does it mean to 'calibrate' an instrument?
choices: Turn it off and on again
Periodically check it against a trusted reference standard and record the result
Set its display to the brightest setting
Replace its batteries
items.explanation: Calibration verifies an instrument against a known, traceable reference and records when it was checked. It is how you keep trusting a tool's numbers over time, and why instruments carry a 'cal due' date.

items.prompt: Two independent error sources of ±2% and ±1.5% combine to a total uncertainty of about:
items.explanation: Independent uncertainties add in quadrature (root-sum-square): sqrt(2^2 + 1.5^2) = sqrt(6.25) = 2.5%. Simply adding them to 3.5% overstates the real uncertainty.

items.prompt: You see harmonics on the analyzer and wonder if they are real or made up by the instrument's own front end overloading. A good check is to:
choices: Add some input attenuation; real harmonics stay the same relative size, instrument-made ones shrink
Increase the display brightness
Switch to the resistance setting
Assume all harmonics are always real
items.explanation: If you reduce the power into the analyzer (more attenuation) and the harmonics shrink relative to the fundamental, they were generated inside the overloaded analyzer, not in your signal. Trust, but verify against a change you control.

items.prompt: You need to find an unwanted 50/60 Hz hum and faint distortion tones hiding in an audio signal. The best instrument is:
choices: A multimeter on DC volts
A spectrum analyzer, because it shows each frequency component separately
A bench power supply
A continuity tester
items.explanation: Hum and distortion are specific frequency components. A spectrum analyzer lays out each tone separately so you can see the hum spike and distortion harmonics directly, which is far harder to spot in the time-domain scope view.
